## BTQ Bitcoin Quantum

- **Reference ID**: BTQ Bitcoin Quantum
- **Title**: BTQ Bitcoin Quantum
- **Authors**: Blockchain & DLT Protocols

- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: BTQ Technologies demonstrates Bitcoin Quantum Core 0.2, a quantum-safe Bitcoin fork replacing ECDSA with NIST-standardized ML-DSA signatures to protect against quantum threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now Decrypt Later; long-range attacks; cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs); Grover's algorithm; double-spend attacks
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Q4 2025 testnet and security audit; Q1 2026 enterprise pilots; Q2 2026 mainnet with migration tools; 2026-2027 integration with exchanges and wallets
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Olivier Roussy Newton, CEO of BTQ Technologies, described the quantum threat as an existential risk and explained the production-ready code protecting Bitcoin's value
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Bitcoin Quantum Core 0.2; Bitcoin Quantum
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology); QuINSA (Quantum Internet Standards Alliance)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDSA; Grover's algorithm
- **Key Takeaways**: Bitcoin Quantum Core 0.2 replaces vulnerable ECDSA signatures with NIST-approved ML-DSA to secure the full transaction lifecycle; Approximately 6.65 million BTC are immediately attackable due to exposed public keys requiring urgent migration; Block size increased to 64 MiB to accommodate larger post-quantum signatures; The BTQ Foundation is established to coordinate industry-wide standards and migration frameworks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit post-quantum security; FIPS 204 standardized ML-DSA
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Block size increased to 64 MiB; PQC signatures are 38-72x larger than ECDSA; ~10-minute mempool window vulnerability
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, CISO, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: None detected
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: digital-assets; Algorithms; Threats; Timeline; Leaders

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: record-now, decrypt-later attack; large-scale quantum computers
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite (CNSA) 2.0 for National Security System owners and operators
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jake Massimo: Applied Scientist on the AWS Cryptography team focusing on post-quantum migration; Nevine Ebeid: Senior Applied Scientist at AWS Cryptography focusing on algorithms development, machine-level optimizations, and FIPS 140-3 requirements
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: AWS-LC; s2n-tls; aws-lc-rs; ACCP 2.0; rustls
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; Cloud KMS; PKI
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); IETF

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; Diffie-Hellman (DH); Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH); Curve x25519; EdDSA; Ed25519; SHA-3; SHAKE; AES-256-GCM; AES-256-XTS; SecP256r1
- **Key Takeaways**: AWS-LC FIPS 3.0 is the first open-source module with FIPS 140-3 validated ML-KEM support; Organizations should adopt hybrid key exchange combining ECDH or X25519 with ML-KEM to mitigate record-now, decrypt-later attacks; ML-KEM-768 is recommended for general use while ML-KEM-1024 supports CNSA 2.0 requirements; Performance improvements include up to 94% faster RSA signatures and 360% faster AES-256-XTS encryption on specific hardware
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST security category 1 (ML-KEM-512); NIST security category 3 (ML-KEM-768); NIST security category 5 (ML-KEM-1024); ML-KEM-512 public key 800 bytes; ML-KEM-768 public key 1184 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 public key 1568 bytes
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid key exchange approach; hybrid post-quantum cipher suites; X25519MLKEM768; SecP256r1MLKEM768; hybrid ECDHE-ML-KEM key agreement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-KEM-512 public key 800 bytes, private key 1632 bytes, ciphertext 768 bytes; ML-KEM-768 public key 1184 bytes, private key 2400 bytes, ciphertext 1088 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 public key 1568 bytes, private key 3168 bytes, ciphertext 1568 bytes; RSA signatures on Graviton2 81 percent faster for 2048-bit; EdDSA signing throughput increased by average of 108 percent; AES-256-GCM increase on average 115 percent; AES-256-XTS encrypting 256 B input 360 percent faster on Intel Ice Lake
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Compliance Officer; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AWS-LC FIPS 3.0 module; s2n-tls library; enable X25519MLKEM768 or SecP256r1MLKEM768 curves; Intel 3rd Gen Xeon for AVX512 IFMA instructions
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Compliance, Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, kms-pqc

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA, SHA256WithRSA, SHA256WithECDSA, P-256, P-521
- **Key Takeaways**: EJBCA 9.1 replaces candidate algorithms with NIST approved ML-KEM and ML-DSA; Signing large CRLs requires Externalμ-ML-DSA support which varies by HSM vendor and firmware; Composite keys combining classical and post-quantum algorithms are supported for migration; KEM keys cannot be used for signature generation requiring CRMF or CMP for proof of possession; HSM support for PQC is vendor-specific and not universally available.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-44 (128 bit), ML-DSA-65 (>128 <192 bit), ML-DSA-87 (256 bit), Falcon-512 (128 bit), Falcon-1024 (256 bit), LMS/HSS (128 bit), SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s to SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256f (128-256 bit), ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Composite keys combining classical cryptographic key with post-quantum key; composite certificates
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: EJBCA 9.1+ or SignServer 6.0+; HSM model and firmware version supporting selected algorithms; Externalμ-ML-DSA support for large CRLs; CRMF or CMP for KEM key proof of possession
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Migrate, hsm-pqc, pki-workshop, hybrid-crypto

## AWS s2n-tls

- **Reference ID**: AWS s2n-tls
- **Title**: AWS s2n-tls
- **Authors**: TLS/SSL Implementation Software
- **Publication Date**: 2026-01-31
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: AWS has deployed ML-KEM hybrid post-quantum key agreement support to KMS, ACM, and Secrets Manager endpoints while phasing out CRYSTALS-Kyber by 2026.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, CRYSTALS-Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: harvest now, decrypt later threats
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Support for CRYSTALS-Kyber continues through 2025; removal across all AWS service endpoints in 2026 in favor of ML-KEM; deployment to all AWS services with HTTPS endpoints over the coming years.
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: aws partition, us-west-2 Region
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Alex Weibel: Senior Software Development Engineer in AWS Cryptography, contributor to s2n-tls, ACCP, and AWS-LC
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: s2n-tls, AWS Libcrypto (AWS-LC), rustls, AWS SDK for Rust, AWS SDK for Java v2, AwsCrtAsyncHttpClient, Amazon Corretto Crypto Provider (ACCP)
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, HTTPS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management Service, Certificate Manager, Secrets Manager, Cloud KMS
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 140-3
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDH, P256, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Customers must update TLS clients and SDKs to offer ML-KEM when connecting to AWS service HTTPS endpoints; Hybrid post-quantum TLS performance impact is negligible (0.05% TPS decrease) when using default SDK settings with connection reuse; CRYSTALS-Kyber support will be removed in 2026 requiring migration to ML-KEM; Enabling hybrid key agreement adds approximately 1600 bytes and 80–150 microseconds per handshake.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519MLKEM768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid post-quantum key agreement, ECDH+ML-KEM hybrid key agreement, graceful fallback to classical key agreement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 1600 additional bytes during TLS handshake; 80–150 microseconds more compute time; 2.3 percent TPS decrease without connection reuse; 0.05 percent TPS decrease with connection reuse
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AWS SDK for Java v2 version 2.30.22 or greater; rustls package with prefer-post-quantum feature flag; AwsCrtAsyncHttpClient configuration with postQuantumTlsEnabled(true); Linux-based platforms for AWS Common Runtime HTTP client
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, hybrid-crypto, kms-pqc, migration-program, pqc-risk-management
- **Source Document**: AWS_s2n-tls.html (1,058,919 bytes, 14,426 extracted chars)

## BoringSSL

- **Reference ID**: BoringSSL
- **Title**: BoringSSL
- **Authors**: TLS/SSL Implementation Software
- **Publication Date**: Continuous
- **Last Updated**: 2026-02-13
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Cloudflare's deployment of hybrid X25519+Kyber post-quantum key agreement for outbound connections to origin servers using TLS 1.3.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Kyber, ML-KEM, Kyber768, Kyber512, X25519Kyber768Draft00
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: store-now/decrypt-later threat
- **Migration Timeline Info**: October 2023: Free plan roll-out aiming for 100% by end of month; Pro and Business aiming for 100% by end of year; Enterprise start February 2024 with 100% by March 2024; Internal connections upgrade completion expected by end of 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Suleman Ahmad, Luke Valenta, Bas Westerbaan
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: BoringSSL, rustls, Chrome
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, TLS 1.2
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Reverse proxy, origin servers, load-balancer, middleboxes
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, AES, P-256, SHA
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid key agreement combining Kyber and X25519 ensures security even if one algorithm is broken; Split ClientHello packets caused by large post-quantum keyshares can break connections with buggy middleboxes; Using HelloRetryRequest allows gradual deployment by advertising support without sending large keyshares initially; Organizations should configure servers to prefer server ciphers and support TLS 1.3 for PQC adoption
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber512 targets AES-128 security level; Kyber768 targets AES-192 security level; X25519Kyber768Draft00
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key agreement (X25519+Kyber); HelloRetryRequest mechanism to avoid split ClientHello issues; GREASE mechanism for unknown codepoints
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: X25519 keyshare 32 bytes; Kyber768 client keyshare 1,184 bytes; Kyber768 server keyshare 1,088 bytes; X25519Kyber768Draft00 client keyshare 1,216 bytes; X25519Kyber768Draft00 server keyshare 1,120 bytes; Kyber768 client ops/sec 31,000; Kyber768 server ops/sec 70,000; X25519Kyber768Draft00 client ops/sec 11,000; X25519Kyber768Draft00 server ops/sec 14,000
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: TLS 1.3 support; BoringSSL library; nginx configuration with ssl_ecdh_curve X25519Kyber768Draft00:X25519; server cipher preference enabled; rustls version 0.21.7 or later for HelloRetryRequest support
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, hybrid-crypto, migration-program, pqc-risk-management, algorithms
- **Source Document**: BoringSSL.html (376,164 bytes, 15,000 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-12T16:45:00

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, elliptic curves, X25519, ECDH secp256r1, ECDH secp384r1, EdDSA, ECDSA, SHA-2, SHAKE256, AES-128, AES-192, AES-256
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC migration must start now to mitigate harvest now decrypt later attacks; RHEL 10.0 provides ML-KEM and ML-DSA as Technology Preview requiring manual policy configuration; Hybrid key exchange combines classic algorithms like X25519 with ML-KEM for TLS 1.3; SLH-DSA signatures are significantly larger and slower than ML-DSA but rely on hash functions; Future versions of RHEL will include PQC support in the DEFAULT crypto-policy
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512 (128 bit), ML-KEM-768 (192 bit), ML-KEM-1024 (256 bit), ML-DSA-44 (Category 1), ML-DSA-65 (Category 3), ML-DSA-87 (Category 5), SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange (X25519MLKEM768, SecP256r1MLKEM768, SecP384r1MLKEM1024), hybrid signatures combining ML-DSA with RSA, ECDSA, or EdDSA
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signature size 7856 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f signature size 17088 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s signature size 16224 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192f signature size 35664 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s signature size 29792 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256f signature size 49856 B; SLH-DSA takes orders of magnitude more processing time to generate and verify than ML-DSA
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Install crypto-policies-pq-preview and crypto-policies-scripts packages; switch system-wide cryptographic policy to DEFAULT:TEST-PQ; install oqsprovider package for ML-DSA in OpenSSL; use TLS 1.3 connections
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Threats, Migrate, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, pki-workshop

- **Key Takeaways**: Nine major cryptographic libraries show varied readiness for PQC adoption as of early 2025; US government mandates require federal agencies to inventory cryptography by May 2023 and transition by 2035; NSA requires quantum-resistant algorithms in NSS applications by 2025; Performance trade-offs and implementation security remain key challenges for real-world adoption; Continued research and coordinated strategies are needed to ensure a secure transition.

- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected

## smallstep Certificate Authority

- **Reference ID**: smallstep Certificate Authority
- **Title**: smallstep Certificate Authority
- **Authors**: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Software
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Smallstep updated its certificate authority and CLI tools to support X25519Kyber768 hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange while noting current limitations of ML-DSA signatures.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now decrypt later attacks; Shor's Algorithm; quantum computers breaking current encryption schemes
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Updated on April 1, 2025; Go 1.24 update planned for August 2025 when Go 1.25 becomes available
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States (US Congress legislation); NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Richard Feynman (seminal 1981 talk on simulating physics with computers)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: step-ca; step-cli
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; X.509
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI; Certificate Authority
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203; FIPS 204; FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519; RSA; ECDSA; EdDSA; ECDH; Diffie-Hellman
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations should enable hybrid TLS handshakes using X25519Kyber768 to defend against harvest now decrypt later attacks; ML-DSA signatures are currently too large for practical certificate issuance; Hybrid certificates combining classical and post-quantum keys ensure compatibility during transition; Users must set GODEBUG=tlsmlkem=1 until Go 1.24 is updated in August 2025; AD CS users should explore alternatives as Microsoft is unlikely to add PQC support
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768; X25519Kyber768; 2048-bit RSA; 3nm node; 2nm node
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange; hybrid X.509 certificates; composite certificates with classical and post-quantum key pairs; gradual introduction of PQ algorithms before removing pre-quantum keys
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA signatures too large for practical use; ML-DSA public keys too large; 2048-bit RSA requires checking approximately 10^308 possible prime factors; Fugaku supercomputer needs millions of years to brute-force 2,048-bit RSA
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; CISO; Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: step-ca v0.28.3+; step-cli v0.28.6+; GODEBUG=tlsmlkem=1 environment variable; TLS client support for X25519MLKEM768; Go 1.24 update planned for August 2025
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto; tls-basics; pki-workshop; migration-program; pqc-risk-management

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: 2025: First PQC use case for software and firmware signing mandated by CNSA 2.0

- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PQMicroLib-Core, PQCryptoLib-Core, PQCryptoLib-SDK, PQPlatform-CoPro, PQPlatform-TrustSys, PQPerform-Flare, PQPerform-Inferno, PQPerform-Flex, PQCryptoLib-Embedded, PQPlatform-Hash, PQPlatform-Lattice, PQPlatform-SubSys, PQPerform

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: CNSA 2.0; FIPS 140-3 CMVP Level 3 or 4; FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, DSA, ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: Secure boot is foundational for cybersecurity and requires immediate action due to long device lifecycles; Hybrid PQ/T schemes are mandated by some governments but increase latency; Stateful signature schemes like LMS and XMSS pose risks for secure boot due to state management challenges; No end-to-end FIPS 140-3 certified PQC solutions currently exist, creating a bottleneck; Flexible hardware and software building blocks allow organizations to mitigate risk now while standards mature.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: PQ/T hybrid approach; Crypto agility; Double transition (hybrid to full PQC)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: PQ/T hybrid doubles latency compared to post-quantum verification alone; PQPerform offers high speed with larger area footprint; PQPlatform optimized for low area footprint.
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Policy Maker, System Integrator
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: FIPS 140-3 CMVP Level 3 or 4 certification (not yet available for PQC); Two-factor authentication mechanism; Hardware accelerators for optimization; State backup mechanisms for stateful signatures.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: crypto-agility, hybrid-crypto, code-signing, iot-ot-pqc, stateful-signatures

## Linux IMA/EVM

- **Reference ID**: Linux IMA/EVM
- **Title**: Linux IMA/EVM
- **Authors**: Secure Boot and Firmware Security
- **Publication Date**: 2025-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Evaluation of integrating quantum-resistant signature algorithms (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, HSS) into the Linux Integrity Measurement Architecture Appraise mechanism.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, HSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Advances in powerful quantum computers threatening classical asymmetric cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Bodies: National Institute of Standards and Technology, Internet Engineering Task Force; Regions: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Johannes Wiesböck, Maximiliane Münch, Michael Weiß
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Botan, Open Quantum Safe Project, hash-sigs, ima-evm-utils, KIWI
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Linux Integrity Measurement Architecture (IMA), Appraise mechanism, secure boot, file system extended attributes
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology, Internet Engineering Task Force
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: NIST FIPS 204, NIST FIPS 205, NIST SP 800-208, RFC 8391, RFC 8554
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, SHA2, SHAKE, elliptic curve signatures
- **Key Takeaways**: Lattice-based algorithms impose close to zero overhead or enhance performance compared to classical methods; Hash-based algorithms can introduce disproportionately high disk and performance overhead; File system mechanisms for storing extended attributes severely influence algorithm compatibility; Short SLH-DSA versions offer competitive performance while fast versions are not recommended due to increased boot times; HSS causes significantly longer boot times negatively impacting user experience
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87, RSA-4096, SLH-DSA-SHA2, SLH-DSA-SHAKE
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Desktop boot time 23.0s to 24.0s with RSA-4096; Fastest boot times around 23.0s with Falcon and ML-DSA-44; HSS average boot time 35s; SHAKE-based SLH-DSA up to 1s slower than SHA2-based versions
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Linux kernel support for extended file attributes; File system compatibility (BTRFS, bcachefs, ext4); IMA policy configuration with appraise_type=imasig; Exclusion of /var/log from appraisal
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, stateful-signatures, code-signing, Assess, migration-program
- **Source Document**: Linux_IMA_EVM.html (307,478 bytes, 15,000 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-12T17:41:52

- **Authors**: Confidential Computing
- **Publication Date**: 2024-10
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The document describes AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV) and its variants (SEV-ES, SEV-SNP, SEV-TIO) as confidential computing solutions that protect data-in-use through memory encryption and trusted execution environments.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TDISP (TEE Device Interface Security Protocol)
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Trusted Execution Environment; Key Management via AMD Secure Processor; Hypervisor isolation
- **Standardization Bodies**: PCI-SIG
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: 128-bit AES XEX; 256-bit AES-XTS
- **Key Takeaways**: SEV-SNP adds memory integrity protection to prevent malicious hypervisor attacks like data replay and memory re-mapping; SEV-TIO extends the Trusted Execution Environment to include PCIe devices using TDISP for authentication and isolation; Memory encryption keys are managed by the AMD Secure Processor and require enablement in both guest OS and hypervisor; Newer AMD EPYC generations support stronger 256-bit AES-XTS encryption and increased thread counts up to 512.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit AES XEX; 256-bit AES-XTS; 128 threads; 15 keys; 256 Threads; 509 Keys; 512 Threads; 1006 Keys; Support for up to 63 Multi Host Keys
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 128 threads; 15 keys; 256 Threads; 509 Keys; 512 Threads; 1006 Keys; Support for up to 63 Multi Host Keys
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Enablement in guest operating system and hypervisor; System BIOS enablement for TSME; AMD EPYC server CPUs (7001 through 9005 generations); SEV Firmware versions specific to CPU family
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: None detected

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC); store now, decrypt later attacks; Shor's algorithm; Grover's algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Current estimates suggest cryptographically relevant quantum computers may emerge in the early to late 2030s; businesses should establish PQC migration strategy now
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST

- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PQC SIM; SINA product family

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC; AES; Shor's algorithm; Grover's algorithm
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must prepare for "store now, decrypt later" attacks by adopting PQC strategies immediately; Quantum computers will break RSA and ECC via Shor's algorithm but only reduce symmetric security via Grover's algorithm which can be mitigated by doubling key lengths; NIST has standardized three PQC algorithms in 2024 (FIPS 203, 204, 205) to guide the transition; G+D and secunet are developing prototypes and market-ready solutions across digital security, financial platforms, and currency technology segments
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Doubling key length for symmetric systems to counteract Grover's algorithm; traditional computers need 100,000 years to solve factorization problems while quantum computers need seconds to days

- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Establish PQC migration strategy now; inventory sensitive data vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks; prepare for NIST standardization finalization

- **Key Takeaways**: XMSS provides security relying only on cryptographic hash function properties without requiring collision resistance; Hash-based signatures are naturally resistant to side-channel attacks and withstand known quantum computer attacks; The document defines both single-tree (XMSS) and multi-tree (XMSS^MT) variants to address slow key generation; WOTS+ is specified as the one-time signature building block for XMSS schemes.

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, sntrup761
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: FreeBSD 15.0 enables quantum-resistant TLS and SSH by default without configuration changes; Hybrid algorithms like X25519MLKEM768 provide belt-and-suspenders security during the transition period; ML-DSA signatures require significantly larger key sizes compared to classical Ed25519; OpenSSH 10 includes automatic keystroke timing obfuscation to prevent side-channel attacks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, ML-DSA-65, mlkem768x25519-sha256, sntrup761x25519-sha512
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid algorithms combining classical and post-quantum key exchange; Fallback chain to classical algorithms for compatibility with older clients
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA-65 private key 5.6 KB; ML-DSA-65 public key 2.7 KB; Ed25519 key 64 bytes; ZFS block cloning ratio 2.00x
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.5+; OpenSSH 10+; FreeBSD 15.0-RELEASE amd64; Enable pkgbase repository via /usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/FreeBSD-base.conf

## ChromeOS

- **Reference ID**: ChromeOS
- **Title**: ChromeOS
- **Authors**: Operating Systems
- **Publication Date**: 2025-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-04
- **Document Status**: Partially Verified
- **Main Topic**: Google Chrome is switching to ML-KEM hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange in version 131, while NIST finalizes PQC standards and a side-channel flaw affects Infineon-based YubiKey devices.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA, XMSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Chrome version 131 release in early November 2024; FN-DSA finalization later this year
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: David Adrian, David Benjamin, Bob Beck, Devon O'Brien (Chrome Team); Thomas Roche (NinjaLab); Victor Lomne
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Chrome web browser, SymCrypt, BoringSSL
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, FIDO
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM, security microcontrollers, firmware signing
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Common Criteria
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, ECDSA, RSA, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Google is abandoning KYBER for ML-KEM due to incompatibility in final versions; Hybrid post-quantum key exchange codepoints are changing from 0x6399 to 0x11EC; Infineon microcontrollers have a side-channel flaw allowing ECDSA private key extraction; Vulnerable YubiKey devices cannot be patched and must be replaced or deprecated; Transition to PQC is a complex, multi-year iterative process requiring careful planning
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM768+X25519, Kyber768+X25519
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid ML-KEM, hybrid post-quantum key exchange, composite certificates (implied by hybrid approach), crypto agility (implied by flag/policy)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, CISO, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Chrome version 131; PostQuantumKeyAgreementEnabled flag; enterprise policy configuration; YubiKey firmware f5.7 or YubiHSM 2.4 for Infineon library deprecation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, migration-program, pqc-risk-management
- **Source Document**: ChromeOS.html (164,078 bytes, 9,393 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-12T19:41:08

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptographically relevant quantum computers; emerging threats to public-key cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: US West (N. California); Europe (Milan)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jake Massimo; Mayank Ambaliya; Panos Kampanakis

- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; HSM; Cloud KMS

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDSA; SHAKE256; HMAC-SHA256
- **Key Takeaways**: AWS KMS now supports ML-DSA key creation and signing operations in US West (N. California) and Europe (Milan); Messages larger than 4096 bytes require external pre-digesting using the EXTERNAL_MU message type; Three ML-DSA key specs (ML_DSA_44, ML_DSA_65, ML_DSA_87) offer NIST security levels 1, 3, and 5 respectively; Post-quantum signatures are critical for long-lived roots of trust in firmware and embedded devices; Local signature verification is possible using OpenSSL 3.5 with public keys retrieved from AWS KMS
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST Level 1 (ML_DSA_44); NIST Level 3 (ML_DSA_65); NIST Level 5 (ML_DSA_87); ML_DSA_SHAKE_256; 128-bit security; 192-bit security; 256-bit security
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML_DSA_44 Public key 1312 bytes; ML_DSA_44 Private key 2560 bytes; ML_DSA_44 Signature 2420 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Public key 1952 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Private key 4032 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Signature 3309 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Public key 2592 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Private key 4896 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Signature 4627 bytes; RAW message limit 4096 bytes; External mu digest size 64 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.5; AWS CLI; FIPS-140-3 Level 3 certified HSMs; pre-processing for messages larger than 4096 bytes
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: kms-pqc, hsm-pqc, code-signing, api-security-jwt, algorithms

- **Key Takeaways**: Do not rely on this experimental fork to protect sensitive data; use hybrid cryptography combining quantum-safe and traditional algorithms for deployment prior to standardization; test interoperability using this fork as a common third-party reference; wait for NIST standardization project outcomes before finalizing deployments.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: L1, L3, L5, bike-l1-sha512, bike-l3-sha512, bike-l5-sha512, classic-mceliece-348864-sha256, frodokem-640-aes-sha256, hqc-128-sha256, kyber-512-sha256, mlkem512-sha256, mlkem768-sha256, mlkem1024-sha384, ntruprime-sntrup761-sha512, dilithium2, dilithium3, dilithium5, falcon512, falcon1024, mayo1, mayo2, mayo3, mayo5, mldsa44, mldsa65, mldsa87, slhdsapuresha2128f, slhdsasha2128ssimple, slhdsashake128fsimple, slhdsashake128ssimple, slhdsasha2192fsimple, slhdsasha2192ssimple, slhdsashake192fsimple, slhdsashake192ssimple, slhdsapuresha2256f, slhdsasha2256ssimple, slhdsashake256fsimple, slhdsashake256ssimple
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange algorithms combining quantum-safe algorithms with traditional public key algorithms like RSA or elliptic curves; hybrid digital signature algorithms combining quantum-safe signatures with RSA3072 or ECDSA using NIST curves.
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: McEliece requires building oqs-openssh with a large stack provision (LDFLAGS="-Wl,--stack,20000000"); HQC algorithms have large stack usage and may cause failures on threads or in constrained environments.
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSH version 10.2; working C compiler; standard library and headers; libcrypto from LibreSSL or OpenSSL; zlib for transport compression; libfido2 for FIDO security token support; large stack provision (LDFLAGS="-Wl,--stack,20000000") for McEliece algorithms.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, crypto-agility, migration-program

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computing risk to cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected

- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Panos Kampanakis (AWS), Marc Manzano (SandboxAQ), Deirdre Connolly (SandboxAQ), Jayati Dev (Comcast)

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, SHA-512, SHA-1, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: SSH protocols currently lack an official IETF maintenance WG for PQ integration but rely on Expert Review codepoint assignments; Large PQC signatures in TLS over TCP and QUIC may exceed initial congestion windows causing extra round-trip times; UEFI specifications allow multiple signatures enabling hybrid classical and quantum-resistant firmware signing; FIDO2 faces challenges implementing hybrid algorithms on constrained devices like NFC tokens; DNSsec requires research into fragmentation due to large PQC key and signature sizes.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Quantum-resistant hybrid key exchanges, Multiple signatures for verifiers, Hybrid classical-PQ cryptographic algorithms
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA authentication data >15KB; TCP initial congestion window ~14KB (initwnd=10); QUIC Amplification window ~4KB; Packet pacing slowdown ~65ms per handshake; kInitialRtt default 333ms
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: IANA Registry Expert Review codepoint assignments; Third-party open-source implementations for EDK II; Increased initial congestion window values; Packet pacing adjustments in QUIC
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, crypto-agility, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, digital-id, stateful-signatures, code-signing

## Cisco Secure Client (AnyConnect)

- **Reference ID**: Cisco Secure Client (AnyConnect)
- **Title**: Cisco Secure Client (AnyConnect)
- **Authors**: VPN and IPsec Software
- **Publication Date**: 2024-06
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The document outlines the transition to post-quantum cryptography, detailing quantum threats, algorithm comparisons, and the impact of hybrid implementations on network infrastructure like NGFWs.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium, Classic McEliece, XMSS, SPHINCS+, Rainbow, SIKE, Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC), harvest now decrypt later (HDNL), Shor's algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Cisco's quantum-safe roadmap targets 2027 for ML-KEM hybrid IKEv2 in Secure Client; Google Chrome enabled ML-KEM by default in May 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Richard Feynman (suggested quantum systems simulation), David Deutsch (formalized theoretical model for quantum computers), Gurdeep Gill (Author)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Cisco Secure Client, Google Chrome
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, QUIC, IKEv2, IPsec, SSL
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs), VPN tunnels, deep packet inspection
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: CNSA 2.0
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES, RSA, ECC, SHA, Diffie-Hellman, DES, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid cryptography combines classical and post-quantum algorithms to provide dual-layer security against current and future threats; Larger key sizes in PQC implementations like ML-KEM can cause packet fragmentation and impact NGFW performance; Organizations must ensure cryptographic agility to seamlessly integrate new algorithms as quantum computing advances; The harvest now decrypt later risk necessitates immediate transition to PQC to protect sensitive data intercepted today.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024, ML-KEM-768 (implied via X25519MLKEM768 group identifier 4588)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid Key Exchange, X25519 + Kyber combinations, cryptographic agility
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: X25519 public key 32 bytes; X25519 ciphertext 32 bytes; Kyber-512 public key ~800 bytes; Kyber-512 ciphertext ~768 bytes; Kyber-768 public key 1,184 bytes; Kyber-768 ciphertext 1,088 bytes; Kyber-1024 public key 1,568 bytes; Kyber-1024 ciphertext 1,568 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-512 public key ~832 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-512 ciphertext ~800 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-768 public key 1,216 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-768 ciphertext 1,120 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-1024 public key 1,600 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-1024 ciphertext 1,600 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, CISO, Compliance Officer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Simultaneous client and headend (ASA/FTD) upgrade for Cisco Secure Client; NGFW firmware or software updates to support new cryptographic algorithms; hardware upgrades or optimizations for NGFWs to handle increased computational load
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Compliance, Migrate, Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, crypto-agility, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc
- **Source Document**: Cisco*Secure_Client\_\_AnyConnect*.html (86,601 bytes, 15,000 extracted chars)

- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Samsung Research explores integrating Post-Quantum Cryptography into 5G and 6G cellular networks to secure primary authentication procedures against quantum threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Kyber, ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers cracking asymmetric algorithms like RSA and ECC; breaking 2048-bit RSA keys in nearly hundred seconds
- **Migration Timeline Info**: No GA PQC release as of Q1 2026; transition expected for 6G era
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: US, Japan, South Korea; Bodies: NIST, 3GPP
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Ramesh Chandra Vuppala; Neha Sharma; Donghyun Je
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: Primary authentication procedure in 5G; SUPI encryption
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key encapsulation mechanism; shared key generation
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, 3GPP, ETSI
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECC, AES
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid cryptography combining classical and PQC algorithms reduces migration risk and ensures backward compatibility; PQC-based shared key generation offers up to 6.7x speed-up compared to classical methods in 5G primary authentication; SUPI encryption using asymmetric keys is vulnerable to quantum attacks and requires PQC integration; Collaboration among industry, government, and standardization bodies is vital for establishing robust migration standards
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2048-bit RSA; 128-bit AES key size
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid cryptography mechanism combining legacy asymmetric cryptography and PQC key encapsulation; hybrid encryption re-encrypting SUPI with classical and PQC algorithms
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 6.7x speed-up with PQC-based shared key generation compared to classical key generation; no additional CPU processing overhead for proposed hybrid solution
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Researcher, Developer, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: x86 platform for simulations; standardized quantum-safe protocols; evaluation of computational efficiency and impact on protocols
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: 5g-security, hybrid-crypto, Algorithms, Threats, Migrate
- **Source Document**: Samsung_Networks_5G_Core.html (63,181 bytes, 13,646 extracted chars)

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, ML-DSA, Falcon
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Future quantum computing attacks; breaking 256-bit elliptic curve signatures; exposure of legacy address formats revealing public keys
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Plausible window for cracking 256-bit digital signatures by the mid-2030s; IBM plans 100,000-qubit chipsets by end of decade; PsiQuantum targets one million photonic qubits by end of decade

- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Ed25519, SHA-256, 256-bit elliptic curve signatures
- **Key Takeaways**: Aptos prioritizes conservative hash-based security over raw performance to avoid untested mathematical assumptions; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s offers robustness with significant signature size and verification speed overheads compared to Ed25519; The rollout is optional and phased via feature flags, allowing selective adoption without forced migration; Industry estimates suggest a threat horizon for breaking 256-bit signatures by the mid-2030s requiring proactive preparation
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s; 48-byte secret keys; 32-byte public keys; 7,856 bytes signature size; 294 microseconds verification time; 256-bit elliptic curve signatures
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Optional activation layer alongside default Ed25519; phased rollout via feature flags; selective adoption by users
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Signatures measure 7,856 bytes (82 times larger than Ed25519); verification takes approximately 294 microseconds (4.8 times slower than Ed25519); Falcon signatures around 1.5 KB
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Policy Maker, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Feature flags to coordinate rollout; infrastructure adjustments for validators, indexers, wallets, and developer tools

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: PCI DSS
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDSA, RSA 2048, SHA-1, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: ACME must evolve to handle hybrid certificates combining classical and post-quantum algorithms; Current ACME clients are incompatible with PQC due to library limitations and payload size constraints; Hybrid CSR generation requires new validation processes for SPKI structures and algorithm combinations; Organizations face significant challenges in automating key rotation across distributed infrastructure including edge and IoT devices
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber 768, RSA 2048
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid TLS, hybrid certificates, composite CSR merging classical and post-quantum algorithms, dual-key generation (ECDSA and PQC)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Kyber keys are an order of magnitude larger than ECDSA; Hybrid SPKI is several times heavier than classical structures; CSR size exceeds previous boundaries; Larger handshake packets trigger buffer issues on IoT devices
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, DevOps Engineer, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.x; updated cryptographic libraries; modernized DevOps pipelines; compatible K8s controllers; HSM resources supporting PQC; updated edge devices

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519Kyber768, X25519Kyber1024, X25519+ML-KEM-768, P-256+ML-KEM-768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: sntrup761x25519-sha512@openssh.com, X25519Kyber768, X25519+ML-KEM-768, P-256+ML-KEM-768
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Bounded memory per flow ~24KB during handshake; Handshake metadata <6KB per flow; Sub-100ms export latency in live mode
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: CMake 3.16+; libpcap-dev; nDPI 4.11+; AF_PACKET TPACKET_V3 support; sudo or cap_net_raw/cap_net_admin capabilities

- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The MLDS-B235 IP Core implements the NIST Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard (ML-DSA) derived from CRYSTALS-Dilithium for FPGA, SoC, and ASIC technologies.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: large-scale quantum computers
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: MLDS-B235; ML-KEM Crypto Core; PQC ML-DSA Crypto Core; Owlet RNG Analyzer
- **Protocols Covered**: MACsec; IPsec; TLS; SSL
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public Key Infrastructures
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204; FIPS 140-3
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA3-256; AES-192; AES-256; SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: The MLDS-B235 IP Core supports NIST security levels 2, 3, and 5 via ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, and ML-DSA-87 parameter sets; The core includes specific countermeasures against Side-Channel Attacks (SCA), timing, and Simple Power Analysis (SPA); Hedged variant signing is supported to improve message data confidentiality using a random seed; The IP Core is portable to AMD (Xilinx), Intel (Altera), and Microchip (Microsemi) devices with AMBA AXI interfaces.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Security Level 2 (ML-DSA-44); Security Level 3 (ML-DSA-65); Security Level 5 (ML-DSA-87)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Key Generation: 16,200 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 10,250 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 7,100 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Signing Average: 1,650 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 1,000 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 850 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Verifying: 11,150 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 7,600 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 5,200 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Resource usage: 10.2K LUT / 8.1K Register (AMD Xilinx Spartan-7); 18.6K LE / 8.0K Register (Intel Altera MAX 10)
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; Hardware Engineer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AMD (Xilinx), Intel (Altera), or Microchip (Microsemi) device; AMBA AXI interfaces; ANSI C drivers; encrypted netlist for one device family
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Compliance, digital-id, entropy-randomness, hsm-pqc

## Alibaba Cloud GVSM

- **Reference ID**: Alibaba Cloud GVSM
- **Title**: Alibaba Cloud GVSM
- **Authors**: Cloud Hardware Security Module (HSM)
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Alibaba Cloud GVSM is a Post-Quantum Cryptography General Virtual Security Module within its Cloud HSM service that integrates NIST-standardized PQC algorithms to protect data against quantum threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, LMS, XMSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later; attacks from mature quantum computers breaking public key encryption
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Chinese mainland; National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: GVSM PQC version; Cloud Hardware Security Module (HSM); Post-Quantum Cryptography General Virtual Security Module (GVSM)
- **Protocols Covered**: PKCS#11; Java Cryptography Extension (JCE); Cryptography API: Next Generation (CNG)
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cloud Hardware Security Module (HSM); Public Key Infrastructure (PKI); Key Management Service
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS-203; FIPS-204; FIPS-205; RFC 8554; RFC 8391
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: Migrate to hardware security modules (HSMs) that resist quantum attacks to defend against Harvest Now, Decrypt Later threats; Use stateful hash-based schemes like LMS and XMSS only with proper signature key state management; Conduct stress tests before migrating business to GVSM PQC version due to potential performance impacts; Deploy quantum-resistant certificate authorities to ensure digital certificates remain tameproof in the quantum era.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: FIPS-203 ML-KEM; FIPS-204 ML-DSA; FIPS-205 SLH-DSA; Maximum number of keys 256 for all listed algorithms

- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Enable TransferSecurityPolicy-2025-03 or TransferSecurityPolicy-FIPS-2025-03 in SFTP endpoint; build OQS OpenSSH or wolfSSH with liboqs; use PEM-encoded or DER-encoded private keys for authentication

- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: Transition to PQC is necessary before cryptographically-relevant quantum computers exist due to harvest now decrypt later threats; .NET 10 abandons the AsymmetricAlgorithm base class for PQC due to incompatible KeySize semantics; New algorithm classes use Span-based methods with fixed buffer sizes defined by FIPS specifications; ML-KEM replaces RSA Key Transport and EC-Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement while ML-DSA and SLH-DSA replace RSA and EC-DSA signatures
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Composite ML-DSA for use in X.509 Public Key Infrastructure
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA-44 Private Key 2560 bytes; ML-DSA-44 Public Key 1312 bytes; ML-DSA-44 Signature 2420 bytes; ML-DSA-65 Private Key 4032 bytes; ML-DSA-65 Public Key 1952 bytes; ML-DSA-65 Signature 3309 bytes; ML-DSA-87 Private Key 4896 bytes; ML-DSA-87 Public Key 2592 bytes; ML-DSA-87 Signature 4627 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect

- **Key Takeaways**: SimpleX Chat mitigates "record now, decrypt later" attacks by integrating Streamlined NTRU Prime into the double ratchet algorithm; Message size privacy is protected by padding all messages to a fixed block size rather than using space-efficient schemes like Padme; Full-stack repudiation (deniability) will be enabled in v5.7 by removing cryptographic signatures from client-client authorization; Break-in recovery is maintained by restricting profiles to a single device to prevent key rotation conflicts; End-to-end encryption security relies on two-factor key exchange via secondary channels to mitigate man-in-the-middle attacks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid integration of Streamlined NTRU Prime with Signal double ratchet algorithm
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Messages padded to a fixed block size; Padme scheme deemed ineffective for mitigating message size attacks

- **Implementation Prerequisites**: SimpleX Chat v5.6 beta or later; Single device usage per profile required for break-in recovery; Secondary communication channel required for two-factor key exchange

## CoreDNS PQC DNSSEC

- **Reference ID**: CoreDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Title**: CoreDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Authors**: DNS Security
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-01
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Integration and performance evaluation of five post-quantum signature algorithm families into CoreDNS to enable quantum-resistant DNSSEC functionality.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, FALCON, SPHINCS+, MAYO, SNOVA, HQC, CRYSTALS-KYBER, CRYSTALS-ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Rainbow-Ia, RedGeMSS128, XMSS, SQISign
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Vulnerability of public-key cryptography based on number factorization or discrete logarithm to quantum attacks; threat to RSA and ECDSA algorithms
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Experts estimate quantum computer capability could occur in the next 10 years; NIST published ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA standards on August 13, 2024; HQC selection announced March 2025
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Julio Gento Suela; Javier Blanco-Romero; Florina Almenares Mendoza; Daniel Díaz-Sánchez; van Rijswijk-Deij; Müller; Shrishak; Shulman; Beernink; Rawat; Jhanwar; Schutijser; Goertzen
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: CoreDNS, BIND9, Unbound, PowerDNS, liboqs, OQS-BIND, PATAD testbed
- **Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC, DNS, UDP, TCP, TLS, HTTPS, gRPC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205, RFC 8624, RFC 4033, RFC 4034, RFC 4035, RFC 5702, RFC 7766
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, ECC, ED25519, SHA-256, P256
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC algorithms introduce operational overhead with signature sizes up to 50 times larger than traditional algorithms; Falcon-512 is the only candidate identified that can be adopted without protocol modifications in some contexts; MAYO-2 outperforms RSA-1280 in signing performance while Falcon-512 performs comparably; Increased signature sizes often force the use of TCP transport, introducing additional latency; Hardware acceleration on x86-64-v3 architectures shows significant improvements for PQC algorithms compared to v2
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768 (mentioned as standard), ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512, SPHINCS+-SHA-256-128s, MAYO-1, MAYO-2, SQISign-1, RSA-1280, 1,232-byte UDP limit
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Out-of-band key distribution; QNAME-based fragmentation (QBF); cipher-suite negotiation mechanisms; dual-stack deployment implied by TCP fallback requirement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: PQC signatures and keys can be between 10 and 50 times larger than traditional algorithms; QBF resolves queries in 43±1ms compared to 83±1ms for standard DNS with TCP fallback; Unbound implementation demonstrated approximately 30% overhead
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: CoreDNS plugin architecture; Go programming language environment; Kubernetes clusters; x86-64-v3 hardware for optimal performance; liboqs integration for some implementations
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Migrate, Assess, Performance, Migration-program
- **Source Document**: CoreDNS_PQC_DNSSEC.html (93,839 bytes, 15,000 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-13T01:31:37

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Shor's Algorithm; factoring integers; breaking RSA

- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Neal H. Walfield (author); Clemens Lang (Crypto Team Product Owner at Red Hat); Devan Carpenter, Justus Winter, Malte Meiboom (Sequoia contributors); Daiki Ueno, Jakub Jelen, Peter Vrabec, Roberto Hueso Gomez, Simo Sorce, Stanislav Zidek (Red Hat contributors)

- **Key Takeaways**: OpenPGP pairs post-quantum algorithms with traditional ones to mitigate risk if one is broken; SLH-DSA offers higher security confidence but larger artifacts and slower performance than ML-DSA; Organizations should use dual certificates for compatibility during the transition period; Post-quantum certificates require RFC 9580 format except for specific encryption subkeys; Implementation currently requires OpenSSL backend and pre-release software versions
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-65 equivalent to AES-192; ML-DSA-87 equivalent to AES-256; SLH-DSA-128s equivalent to AES-128; SLH-DSA-256s equivalent to AES-256; ML-KEM-768 equivalent to AES-192; ML-KEM-1024 equivalent to AES-256
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid algorithms pairing PQC with classical (e.g., ML-DSA-65+Ed25519); Dual certificate strategy using one RSA and one PQC certificate for signing; Partial transition via subkeys in old format
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SLH-DSA signatures 8 kilobytes to 30 kilobytes; ML-DSA-65 signature 3.3 kilobytes; ML-DSA-87 signature 4.6 kilobytes; Ed25519 signature 64 bytes; Generated PQC certificate 42 kilobytes; Elliptical cryptography certificate 2.3 kilobytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL backend; sequoia-openpgp version 2.2.0-pqc.1; sq version 1.4.0-pqc.1; RFC 9580 profile; cargo build with crypto-openssl feature
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Hybrid-crypto, Code-signing, Email-signing, Migration-program

- **Key Takeaways**: WinSCP supports hybrid key exchange methods combining lattice-based algorithms with classical elliptic curve methods; GSSAPI-based key exchange enables automatic credential re-delegation for long-running sessions; Data-based rekeys are critical to prevent 32-bit packet sequence number wraparound attacks; Users should avoid legacy 1024-bit Diffie-Hellman groups due to security warnings; Key exchange performance may impact connection startup time on slower machines.

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Key exchange may take several tens of seconds on slow machines; Max data before rekey recommended at 1 gigabyte; Max time before rekey recommended at 60 minutes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: SCP or SFTP file protocol selection; Kerberos V5 credentials for GSSAPI authentication

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC), Harvest Now Decrypt Later (HNDL), Shor's algorithm, Grover's quantum algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: US federal government migration by 2035; UK inventory and plan by 2028 with full transition by 2035; Japan and Canada full transition by 2035; Australia transition by 2030; EU critical infrastructure by 2030 with full transition by 2035; TLS 1.3 support required by 2030
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States, UK, Japan, Canada, Australia, European Union, Netherlands
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Scott Piper, Yali Gottlib, Addi Grinbaum

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDH, ECDSA, EdDSA, AES-128, SHA-2, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must inventory cryptographic mechanisms vulnerable to quantum attacks immediately; Upgrading TLS and SSH key exchange functionality is the most urgent and easiest step to mitigate Harvest Now Decrypt Later risks; Migration plans should be broken into three parts: inventory, migrate TLS/SSH, and migrate everything else; PQC support requires adherence to NIST standards published in August 2024 or later
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: RSA-2048, AES-128, SHA-2, less than a million noisy qubits required to break RSA-2048
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid cryptography, quantum-safe hybrid cryptography
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 84% of accessible domains support TLS 1.3; 45% of total accessible domains support PQC key exchanges; largest number factored by quantum computer is 21 (or 15); Google Willow chip has 105 qubits
- **Target Audience**: CISO, Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Operations

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Shor's algorithm; quantum supremacy; harvest now decrypt later (key-agreement hoarding); retrospective attacks on stored messages
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Standardization exercise expected to finish by 2024; Huawei plans to introduce algorithms in advance of the 2024 deadline
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Craig Gidney; Martin Ekera; Barbara Terhal

- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must transition to quantum-safe algorithms immediately to prevent retrospective attacks on stored key-agreement messages; Hybrid schemes combining legacy and quantum-safe mechanisms mitigate risks of algorithm breakage during the standardization period; Symmetric-key algorithms like AES-256 remain secure against quantum computers without replacement; Implementation of quantum-safe algorithms requires optimization due to large key sizes and bandwidth consumption
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2048-bit RSA; twenty million qubits; fifty qubits; AES-256
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid scheme implementing both Diffie-Hellman and a candidate quantum-safe key-exchange mechanism
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Very large public key for McEliece; compact key sizes for super-singular isogeny-based algorithms; very small signature size for multivariate algorithms; consumption of bandwidth; relatively slow speed for super-singular isogeny-based algorithms
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; CISO; Policy Maker; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Careful study of protocols; optimization of algorithms to reduce performance impact

## PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC

- **Reference ID**: PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Title**: PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Authors**: DNS Security
- **Publication Date**: 2024
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-01
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: A field study evaluating the performance and deliverability of experimental Post-Quantum Cryptography algorithms in DNSSEC using PowerDNS and RIPE ATLAS.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Falcon, Dilithium, SPHINCS+, XMSS, XMSSMT
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers breaking current cryptography; Q-day
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jason Goertzen; David Joseph; Peter Thomassen; Peter van Dijk
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PowerDNS; liboqs; oqs-provider; Open Quantum Safe
- **Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC; DNS; TLS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: DNS Infrastructure; Authoritative Server; Recursor
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSASHA256; RSA
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC algorithms with larger signatures like SPHINCS+ and XMSS suffer significantly higher SERVFAIL rates due to packet size limits; Falcon demonstrates the fewest delivery issues due to its small signature size; Switching from UDP to TCP improves response success rates for large PQC signatures; Stateful hash-based signatures require careful parameter set selection based on zone size; Some resolvers incorrectly report DNSSEC validation (AD bit) for unsupported PQC algorithms.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Falcon-512; Dilithium2; XMSS parameter sets; XMSSMT parameter sets
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SERVFAIL rate for XMSS with NSEC3 (TCP, no DO bit) increases from 19.9% to 51.6%; Correct response rates for SPHINCS+ and XMSS increase from around 80% to 90% when switching from UDP to TCP; Dilithium2 correct response rate increases from 89.1% to 93.2% with TCP; Falcon correct response rate increases from 97.4% to 98.5% with TCP
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3 provider; liboqs library; modified PowerDNS DNSSECKeeper; modified BIND 9
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; Playground; stateful-signatures; tls-basics
- **Source Document**: PowerDNS_PQC_DNSSEC.html (72,840 bytes, 13,709 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-13T02:13:49

- **Authors**: Quantum-Safe Blockchain

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA, ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Store Now, Decrypt Later; obsolescence of ECDSA due to increasing computing power
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Q1 2025: Quantum-secure PoW Mainnet live; Q3 2025: Certik Audit complete and Mainnet Launch; H1 2026: TGE & Exchange listings; H2 2027: Mainnet Upgrade for Post-quantum AI Communication Infrastructure

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2^18 effort for MD5 collisions; 2^60 effort for SHA-1 collisions; 2^80 effort for RIPEMD160 collisions; Scrypt N=2^16 to 2^24; Argon2 m=65536; maxmem limit 1GB + 1KB
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 22KB gzipped for full build; 2.4KB for single-hash build; Scrypt N=2^16 takes 0.1s with 64MB RAM; Scrypt N=2^24 takes 27s with 16GB RAM; Argon2 is suggested to be avoided in JS due to lack of fast Uint64Array
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: npm or deno package manager; polyfill for getRandomValues for React Native; manual maxmem adjustment for Scrypt N > 2^20

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC; DH; DHE; ECDHE; AES; chacha20-poly1305
- **Key Takeaways**: Retaining intercepted traffic is economically trivial, shifting defense focus to increasing adversary decryption costs; Encrypted Client Hello forces indiscriminate bulk collection to inflate archive size; Aggressive rekeying and larger key exchange parameters multiply quantum computations required for recovery; TLS 1.3 and QUIC lack in-band ephemeral rekeying, creating a critical protocol gap
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: RSA-2048; Level 3 messaging security (Apple PQ3)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange; Hybrid post-quantum Transport Layer Security
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Petabyte-scale archival costs collapsed by roughly 95% since 2010; 68 exabytes of global data traffic in 2024; Negligible bandwidth overhead for rekeying
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Researcher; Policy Maker; CISO
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Open-source simulation pipeline; Encrypted Client Hello configuration; SSH RekeyLimit configuration; Larger key exchange parameters
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Assess, Migrate, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, pqc-risk-management, hybrid-crypto
